result:
The first thing that comes is wind. Not a metaphor—just actual wind, the kind that rattles the windows at 3:17 a.m. and makes the house feel like it’s breathing. I’m lying here listening to it, thinking about how everything alive eventually learns to make noise when it’s restless. Trees. Oceans. People. Even silence eventually cracks and starts humming.

I keep a notebook by the bed for nights like this. Most pages are just fragments: “the particular shade of tired in my mother’s voice when she says she’s fine,” or “the way my dog sighs like an old man who’s seen every war.” Little captures. Evidence that I was here and paying attention.

There’s a theory that the universe is still trying to decide what it wants to be. 13.8 billion years and it’s still stretching, still unsure. I find that comforting. I’m thirty-six and I still don’t know what I want for breakfast most mornings. Maybe we’re all just doing our best impression of certainty while the fabric of everything keeps expanding faster than light can catch up. Maybe the panic I feel at 2 a.m. is cosmic FOMO. The universe is out there becoming and I’m worried about whether I left the stove on.

My grandfather used to say that every man needs three things: a good pair of boots, a song he can sing when no one’s listening, and one person who knows exactly where all his bodies are buried but loves him anyway. He died before he could tell me which of those he considered most important. I think about that a lot. The things we almost say are sometimes louder than the things we do.

Last week I watched a spider rebuild her web six times in one afternoon. The wind kept destroying it and she kept starting over without apparent rage or despair. Just eight patient legs doing what eight patient legs do. I felt personally attacked by her work ethic. I’ve abandoned three novels, four relationships, and a sourdough starter with far less provocation.

There’s a Japanese concept called *wabi-sabi*—the beauty of impermanence, the grace in things that are broken or incomplete. I’m trying to believe in it. Some days I do. Other days I just want the broken parts to stop being so loud when I’m trying to sleep.

I’ve been thinking about forgiveness lately. Not the soft Instagram version with lavender fields and quotes in cursive. Real forgiveness. The kind that requires you to look at what was done to you—or what you did—and say: this happened. It cannot unhappen. And still, I choose to carry it differently. Not lighter, necessarily. Just... differently. Like switching the grocery bags from one hand to the other. The weight hasn’t changed but at least both arms get a turn at being numb.

My mother once told me that love is mostly just showing up when it’s inconvenient. I was fourteen and thought she was being corny. Now I understand she was being deadly serious. The people who sit in hospital rooms at 4 a.m. The ones who answer the phone when your voice is shaking. The friends who have watched you become every version of yourself and somehow still recognize you. That’s the whole terrifying miracle. Not fireworks. Just presence. Stubborn, annoying, inconvenient presence.

Outside my window the wind is still at it. There’s a plastic bag caught in a tree that’s been there for three weeks now. It flaps and flaps like it’s trying to tell us something urgent. Maybe it is. Maybe the bag is the only one of us brave enough to admit it’s trapped and still refuses to be quiet about it.

I’ve been alive for 13,340 days. That’s not very many, cosmically speaking. The light hitting my face right now left the sun eight minutes ago. By the time you read this, I’ll be someone else. Not dramatically. Just the small changes that happen while words move from one mind to another. A few more gray hairs. A slightly different opinion about something trivial. The version of me writing this is already gone.

That’s okay.

I used to be afraid of that—of disappearing one cell at a time. Now I think it’s kind of beautiful. Like those time-lapse videos of flowers blooming and dying in six seconds. You get to see the whole heartbreaking honest thing from start to finish. No one gets to stay the same. The mercy is we don’t have to.

The wind just died down. Sudden as a held breath. In the new quiet I can hear my own pulse, that stubborn idiot drum still marching. Still saying *yes, again, yes, again, yes.*

I think I’ll get up and make coffee even though it’s too early. I’ll burn the toast on purpose because I like the smell. I’ll water the plants that are definitely judging me for forgetting last week. I’ll probably text someone I love and say something too sincere for 6 a.m. They’ll forgive me. Or they won’t. Either way the day will keep happening.

And I’ll keep happening inside it. Imperfectly. Loudly sometimes. Quietly others. Like the spider and the wind and the plastic bag and every other stubborn thing that refuses to stop becoming.

The notebook is still open. I think I’ll write something true in it before the sun comes up.

*Still here. Still paying attention.* 

That feels like enough for now.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
